Compliance Colocation — Independent Mid-Market Guide — MCA

# Compliance Colocation — Independent Mid-Market Guide

Independent guide to compliance colocation for regulated mid-market companies — HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI DSS, HITRUST, cyber insurance, and the 2026 HIPAA Security Rule update. What your facility actually needs to provide. Free advisory from Metro Colo Advisory.

Get My Free Assessment

- Independent Specialist
- Provider Agnostic
- Free to Clients

## Compliance Colocation — The Independent Mid-Market Guide

For mid-market companies in regulated industries colocation infrastructure is not just an IT decision — it is a compliance decision. The facility you choose becomes part of your own compliance posture. Its certifications appear in your audit documentation. Its controls are evaluated during your SOC 2 audit, your HIPAA risk assessment, your PCI DSS assessment, and your cyber insurance renewal. Choosing the wrong facility creates compliance gaps that are expensive and time-consuming to close.

This independent compliance colocation guide covers every major regulatory framework that affects mid-market colocation decisions nationally. Consider this your independent compliance colocation review — written by an advisor with no financial stake in which provider or facility you choose.

Metro Colo Advisory evaluates compliance colocation across all major US markets and providers — giving regulated mid-market companies the independent analysis their compliance teams need before any facility conversation begins.

Your colocation facility’s compliance certifications directly become part of your own compliance posture. A facility with the wrong certifications — or certifications that do not cover your specific space and services — creates audit failures and regulatory exposure regardless of how well you manage your own environment.

An independent advisor verifies facility compliance posture for your specific requirements before you commit to any contract. Metro Col

results.title: How to Vet Data Center Partners: Checklist
results.author: enterprises.website
results.text:
How to Vet Data Center Partners: Checklist

A practical due-diligence checklist for vetting colocation and wholesale data center partners with power, pipeline, tenant mix, and risk checks.

Choosing a colocation or wholesale data center partner is not a branding exercise. It is a procurement decision that affects uptime, expansion speed, compliance posture, and total cost of ownership for years. The best buyers treat this process like data center due diligence: they verify power availability, inspect the capacity pipeline, assess tenant mix, and pressure-test the operator’s ability to deliver against the contract, not just the sales deck. If you are building an RFP, benchmarking vendors, or comparing regions, it helps to think with the same discipline used by market-intelligence teams in the investment world, where capacity, absorption, supplier activity, and project timing determine risk. For a broader market lens, see our guide on merchant onboarding API best practices for how structured vendor reviews reduce friction, and our overview of building trust in AI security measures for a useful framework on verifying claims before signing.

This guide gives hosting buyers a practical colocation checklist for evaluating data center partners. It is built around the same forward-looking intelligence mindset seen in DC Byte-style market analysis: not just what exists today, but what will be available when you need it. You will learn how to test capacity pipeline credibility, read power guarantees, interpret tenant concentration, and estimate investment risk from both an operational and commercial perspective. If your procurement process includes contract review, you may also find the structure in our article on pricing and contract lifecycle for SaaS e-sign vendors on federal schedules helpful for building a tighter buy-side workflow.

## 1) Start with the Business Requirement, Not the Facility Tour

### Define the workload profile in plain English

Before you compare buil

results.title: Data Center & Colocation Buyer's Guide | Strativant
results.author: Strativant
results.text:
Data Center & Colocation Buyer's Guide | Strativant

✕

Data Center & Colocation

# Data Center & Colocation Buyer's Guide

Cloud isn't always the answer — and a cabinet isn't always cheaper. Here's how to model the decision by workload, read a colo quote line by line, evaluate the facility behind the tour, and negotiate terms that don't bite in year three.

Get My ClarityMap™ → Cloud Advisory

July 4, 2026 7 min read 1318 words

By Atit Shah· Founder & Managing Partner

Somewhere in the last decade, "move it to the cloud" hardened from a strategy into a reflex — and reflexes are expensive. For steady-state workloads, hardware you already own, egress-heavy data patterns, and licensing that punishes virtual cores, a well-negotiated colocation footprint routinely beats cloud economics. For elastic, spiky, or rapidly changing workloads, it routinely loses. The organizations that get this right don't pick a side; they model the decision per workload and let the math choose.

This guide is the buyer's half of that discipline: when colocation genuinely wins, what a colo quote actually contains, how to evaluate the facility behind the sales tour, and the contract terms that quietly compound against you after signature.

## When Colocation Beats Cloud — and When It Doesn't

Colocation tends to win when a workload is predictable: utilization is steady, capacity is known a year out, and you're paying cloud's flexibility premium for flexibility you never use. It wins when data gravity is real — large datasets that move often, where egress and cross-region transfer quietly dominate the bill. It wins when licensing is core-based and hostile to public cloud, when you own capable hardware mid-depreciation, and when sovereignty or latency pins compute to a place.

Cloud wins the mirror image: elastic or unpredictable demand, global reach without building it, managed services that would otherwise be headcount, and experiments you may kill in a quarter. Most organizations land hybrid

results.title: From Anatomy to Action: Navigating Data Center Contracts
results.text:
From Anatomy to Action: Navigating Data Center Contracts

Share

Alert

By Brittney D. Sandler, Philip T. Evans

## Takeaways

Share

Businesses should carefully consider technical, operational and financial details when executing data center contracts.

Share

Appropriately addressing these considerations in data center contracts will help protect each party’s interests and ensure more effective and predictable operations.

10.24.24

Our colleagues recently provided a comprehensive overview of the anatomy of a data center, which explored the structural, energy and real estate implications of these essential facilities.

As more businesses rely on external data centers to house their critical infrastructure, the importance of a well-negotiated data center services agreement becomes paramount. These contracts require more than just finding space—they require careful consideration of the technical, operational and financial details that will support a customer’s business needs.

Although terms may vary depending on whether the contract is with a dedicated data center, shared colocation ("colo") data center, and/or managed services provider (MSP) that subcontracts with a data center to provide space and services, the following are key considerations when negotiating any data center deal.

Space and Specifications: Defining the EnvironmentThe space a customer leases within a data center can vary greatly in size and specifications. When contracting, it is important to detail:

- Size and Power Requirements: How much physical space and power the customer’s equipment will need. Power density (watts per square foot) is critical, and must align with the customer’s anticipated growth. Accordingly, parties should specify initial size and power requirements and negotiate a customer’s rights of first refusal for adjacent space and additional power.
- Cooling and Heating: Climate requirements to ensure that the data center maintains appropriate temperatures for customer equipment
